feedback.text: Hello, As a gamer myself i would like to express my support for this initiative. I have repeatedly seen my friends, online personas and members of the gaming community fall victim to "Dark patterns" and "Addictive practices as mentioned in the initiative. These tactics promote excessive spending in of itself. And often could lead to gambling addictions. I would also like to voice a concern about game publishing companies disabling access to online only games, people already paid for (e.g., The Crew, Anthem), without giving the players any "end-of-life" options. Something as simple as giving players tools to create their own servers or creating an single-player only experiences could solve this problem altogether. Alternatively giving the public access to the source code of the games could give passionate players and communities options to develop and fix the games, with negligible costs on the side of publishers. Another related concern of mine is the growing influence of large payment processors (Visa, MasterCard) in dictating what games can be sold on storefronts that are using their services. For example, Steam has recently been forced to remove a lot of games from their catalogue. Which resulted in a big losses for many customers and some small developers. Games are a form of art, often times created by very passionate teams of developers, trying to express themselves. And it's disheartening to see a lot of games disappearing from the reach of passionate communities. I believe there is a lot more problems in digital space. But I believe issues mentioned here are closely related to the Digital Fairness Act. Thank you for considering my input on this matter.
